edges.description: Enhanced TGF-beta/SMAD signaling drives matrix metalloproteinase upregulation and degradation of the aortic wall extracellular matrix.
Degradation of elastic fibers and collagen weakens the aortic media, promoting aneurysmal dilation.
Progressive medial weakening predisposes to dissection, characteristically at smaller aortic diameters than in Marfan syndrome.

nodes.description: Progressive aortic aneurysm, a hallmark vascular feature. Orphanet classifies aortic aneurysm as Very frequent (99-80%) in LDS.

evidence.reference_title: Aneurysm syndromes caused by mutations in the TGF-beta receptor.
evidence.snippet: The natural history of both types was characterized by aggressive arterial aneurysms (mean age at death, 26.0 years) and a high incidence of pregnancy-related complications (in 6 of 12 women)
HP:0004942 | Aortic aneurysm | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Aortic dissection is a life-threatening complication occurring at smaller aortic diameters than in Marfan syndrome. Orphanet classifies this as Very frequent (99-80%).

evidence.reference_title: Aneurysm syndromes caused by mutations in the TGF-beta receptor.
evidence.snippet: The natural history of both types was characterized by aggressive arterial aneurysms (mean age at death, 26.0 years) and a high incidence of pregnancy-related complications (in 6 of 12 women)
HP:0002647 | Aortic dissection | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Progressive dilation of the aortic root, occurring at earlier ages and smaller diameters than in Marfan syndrome. Dissections have occurred at aortic dimensions of 3.9-4.0 cm, unlike the 5.0 cm threshold in Marfan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: dissections have occurred in individuals with LDS 1, 2, or 3 at aortic dimensions of 3.9-4.0 cm

nodes.description: Abnormally long, slender fingers, a skeletal feature shared with Marfan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001166 | Arachnodactyly | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Dissection can occur in arteries throughout the body, not just the aorta. Orphanet classifies arterial dissection as Very frequent (99-80%).

evidence.snippet: HP:0005294 | Arterial dissection | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Widespread arterial tortuosity, particularly of the head and neck vessels. A distinguishing feature from Marfan syndrome. Increased vertebral arterial tortuosity is a marker of adverse aortic outcome.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and complications of Loeys-Dietz syndrome: A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: the most commonly reported features and complications being: aortic aneurysms and dissections, arterial tortuosity, high arched palate, abnormal uvula and hypertelorism
HP:0005116 | Arterial tortuosity | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Asthma is part of the immunologic/allergic phenotype of LDS.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: LDS has been associated with a high prevalence of immunologic features including asthma, food allergy, eczema, and allergic rhinitis
HP:0002099 | Asthma | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Abnormal scarring pattern reflecting connective tissue fragility.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000987 | Atypical scarring of skin | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Bifid or broad uvula, a hallmark feature often used for clinical screening. May range from broad uvula to overt cleft palate.

evidence.reference_title: Aneurysm syndromes caused by mutations in the TGF-beta receptor.
evidence.snippet: The disease is characterized by the triad of arterial tortuosity and aneurysms, hypertelorism, and bifid uvula or cleft palate
HP:0000193 | Bifid uvula | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Blue sclerae reflecting connective tissue thinning of the sclera.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000592 | Blue sclerae | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Easy bruising reflecting vascular and connective tissue fragility.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000978 | Bruising susceptibility | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Permanent flexion contracture of fingers, part of the skeletal phenotype.

evidence.snippet: HP:0100490 | Camptodactyly of finger | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Cervical spine instability or malformation present in 51% of LDS 1/2 patients. Requires assessment with flexion-extension X-rays.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: Cervical spine findings are prominent features in LDS 1/2 (51%)

nodes.description: Cleft palate, ranging from submucous to overt, is part of the craniofacial spectrum of LDS.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000175 | Cleft palate | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Talipes equinovarus, present in a proportion of individuals.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001762 | Talipes equinovarus | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Premature fusion of cranial sutures, most commonly the sagittal suture but also coronal, metopic, and squamosal. More common in LDS type 1.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: Most commonly, the sagittal suture is prematurely closed, but the coronal, metopic, and squamosal sutures can also be involved
HP:0001363 | Craniosynostosis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Particularly prominent in LDS type 3 (SMAD3 mutations). Most affected individuals present with early-onset osteoarthritis with osteochondritis dissecans, onset as early as age 12.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in SMAD3 cause a syndromic form of aortic aneurysms and dissections with early-onset osteoarthritis.
evidence.snippet: In contrast with other aneurysm syndromes, most of these affected individuals presented with early-onset osteoarthritis

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and complications of Loeys-Dietz syndrome: A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: LDS Type 3 demonstrated an increased prevalence of mitral valve prolapse and arthritis

nodes.description: Eczema is part of the immunologic/allergic phenotype of LDS.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000964 | Eczematoid dermatitis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Eosinophilic infiltration of the esophagus, part of the allergic/ eosinophilic gastrointestinal disease spectrum in LDS.

evidence.snippet: HP:0410151 | Eosinophilic infiltration of the esophagus | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Enhanced TGF-beta signaling leads to upregulation of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and reduced expression of tissue inhibitors, causing degradation of elastic fibers and collagen in the arterial wall. This weakens the vessel wall leading to aneurysm formation and dissection.
High prevalence of food allergies (31% versus 6-8% in general population), with most common allergens being eggs, milk, soy, peanuts, and tree nuts.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: A conservative prevalence estimate of food allergies in this population is 31% (compared with 6-8% prevalence in the general population)

nodes.description: Wide-spaced eyes, part of the characteristic craniofacial gestalt and the diagnostic triad.

evidence.reference_title: Aneurysm syndromes caused by mutations in the TGF-beta receptor.
evidence.snippet: The disease is characterized by the triad of arterial tortuosity and aneurysms, hypertelorism, and bifid uvula or cleft palate
HP:0000316 | Hypertelorism | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: LDS is associated with a high prevalence of immunologic features including food allergy (31%), eosinophilic gastrointestinal disease, asthma, eczema, and allergic rhinitis. TGF-beta pathway disruption affects immune regulation and mucosal tolerance.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: LDS has been associated with a high prevalence of immunologic features including asthma, food allergy, eczema, and allergic rhinitis

nodes.description: Joint dislocation reflecting ligamentous laxity.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001373 | Joint dislocation | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Generalized joint hypermobility, contributing to joint dislocations and chronic pain. Extremity contractures in conjunction with joint hyperextension are unusual in the general population but common in LDS.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: Extremity contractures in conjunction with joint hyperextension are unusual in the general population but common in LDS
HP:0001382 | Joint hypermobility | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Malar flattening is a craniofacial feature of LDS.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000272 | Malar flattening | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Micrognathia (small jaw) is part of the craniofacial phenotype of LDS.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000347 | Micrognathia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Missense mutations affecting the kinase domain of TGFBR2.
Mitral valve insufficiency, an occasional cardiac feature.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001653 | Mitral regurgitation | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Myopia (nearsightedness) is an occasional ocular feature.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000545 | Myopia | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Low bone mineral density and skeletal fragility, with at least 60% of patients having low or very low bone mineral density.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: at least 60% of patients had low or very low bone mineral density in the spine, hip, and/or femoral neck

nodes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in TGF-beta receptors paradoxically lead to increased TGF-beta signaling in the aortic wall, mediated through non-canonical pathways and compensatory upregulation. This results in excessive SMAD2/3 phosphorylation and activation of ERK and p38 MAPK downstream targets that promote extracellular matrix degradation.

evidence.reference_title: Noncanonical TGFβ signaling contributes to aortic aneurysm progression in Marfan syndrome mice.
evidence.snippet: extracellular signal-regulated kinase (ERK) 1 and 2 and Smad2 are activated in a mouse model of MFS, and both are inhibited by therapies directed against TGFbeta

evidence.reference_title: Aneurysm syndromes caused by mutations in the TGF-beta receptor.
evidence.snippet: The disease is characterized by the triad of arterial tortuosity and aneurysms, hypertelorism, and bifid uvula or cleft palate and is caused by heterozygous mutations in the genes encoding transforming growth factor beta receptors 1 and 2 (TGFBR1 and TGFBR2, respectively)

nodes.description: Patent ductus arteriosus is a congenital cardiovascular feature. Orphanet classifies this as Very frequent (99-80%).

evidence.snippet: HP:0001643 | Patent ductus arteriosus | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Pectus excavatum or pectus carinatum, similar to Marfan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: Skeletal features in all types of LDS can show overlap with Marfan syndrome, including pectus deformity, scoliosis, and flat feet
HP:0000767 | Pectus excavatum | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Flat feet, a common skeletal feature shared with Marfan syndrome. Orphanet classifies this as Very frequent (99-80%).

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: Skeletal features in all types of LDS can show overlap with Marfan syndrome, including pectus deformity, scoliosis, and flat feet
HP:0001763 | Pes planus | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: High incidence of pregnancy-related complications including aortic dissection. Among 222 women with 522 pregnancies in a systematic review, 4% experienced aortic dissection with 1% peripartum mortality.

evidence.reference_title: Aneurysm syndromes caused by mutations in the TGF-beta receptor.
evidence.snippet: a high incidence of pregnancy-related complications (in 6 of 12 women)

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and complications of Loeys-Dietz syndrome: A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Amongst 222 women who underwent 522 pregnancies, 4% experienced an aortic dissection and the peripartum mortality rate was 1%

nodes.description: Progressive scoliosis requiring monitoring and potential surgical intervention.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002650 | Scoliosis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: TGF-beta signaling is critical for skeletal development, particularly in suture closure and vertebral column formation. Dysregulated signaling leads to craniosynostosis, scoliosis, and cervical spine instability. Cervical spine findings are prominent in LDS 1/2 (51% of patients).

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: Cervical spine findings are prominent features in LDS 1/2 (51%), but presence in LDS 3 and 4 is unknown

nodes.description: Spontaneous pneumothorax, an occasional pulmonary feature.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002108 | Spontaneous pneumothorax | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Stretch marks (striae) reflecting connective tissue fragility.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001065 | Striae distensae | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Aneurysm syndromes caused by mutations in the TGF-beta receptor.
evidence.snippet: We found a mutation in TGFBR1 or TGFBR2 in all probands with typical Loeys-Dietz syndrome (type I) and in 12 probands presenting with vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (Loeys-Dietz syndrome type II)
TGFBR1 | transforming growth factor beta receptor 1 | hgnc:11772 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

evidence.reference_title: Aneurysm syndromes caused by mutations in the TGF-beta receptor.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in either TGFBR1 or TGFBR2 predispose patients to aggressive and widespread vascular disease
TGFBR2 | transforming growth factor beta receptor 2 | hgnc:11773 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

nodes.description: Tall stature is a frequent skeletal feature of LDS, consistent with the connective tissue disorder spectrum.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000098 | Tall stature | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Thin, velvety, translucent skin with easy bruising and visible veins. Scars may be atrophic and wound healing delayed.

evidence.reference_title: Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: Cutaneous findings in LDS include velvety, thin, translucent skin with easy bruising and visible veins

nodes.description: Uterine rupture during pregnancy, reflecting connective tissue fragility of the uterine wall. Orphanet classifies this as Very frequent (99-80%).

evidence.snippet: HP:0100718 | Uterine rupture | Very frequent (99-80%)
